coredata.dc:title: Kinetic and thermodynamic synergy of spongiform nanostructure and alien dopants enables promoted sodium-ion transfer for high-performance sodium storage
coredata.dc:creator: Diao, Zhidan
Yang, Peijun
Wang, Yiqing
Li, Mingtao
Jin, Hui
Shen, Shaohua
coredata.dc:description: To address the bottlenecks of sluggish sodium-ion transfer processes, the electrode materials of sodium-ion battery (SIB) are always designed from the viewpoints of sodium-ion diffusion kinetics or reaction thermodynamics for high-performance sodium storage. Herein, starting with spongiform TiO2/C composite derived from zinc alginate, a NH4Cl-assisted annealing strategy is developed to prepare chlorine-doped spongiform TiO2/C composite (ZATC-Cl). Acting as the anode of SIBs, the obtained ZATC-Cl delivers excellent sodium storage performance with a high reversible capacity of 352.4 mAh g−1 at 50 mA g−1, a superior rate capability of 246.8 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1 and a considerable high-rate cycling performance of 248.5 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1 for 1000 cycles. It is believed that the unique spongiform structure and ultra-small sized TiO2 particles in ZATC-Cl can achieve fast sodium-ion insertion/extraction, realizing rapid sodium-ion diffusion kinetics. Moreover, as well evidenced by experiment results and theoretical calculations, the Cl dopants introduced in ZATC-Cl can provide more active sites for robust sodium-ion chemical adsorption, optimizing sodium-ion reaction thermodynamics. This study demonstrates an alternative approach to improve the sodium storage capability of TiO2 anodes by the synergistically engineering the morphological and structural properties and manipulating the surface active sites, from both kinetic and thermodynamic viewpoints of sodium storage processes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2/C composite
Diffusion kinetics
Reaction thermodynamics
Sodium storage

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFE0193900) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51961165103). S. Shen was supported by “the Youth Innovation Teams of Shaanxi Universities”, the National Program for Support of Top-notch Young Professionals and the “Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.”

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The comparisons of sodium-ion transfer (a) kinetics and (b) thermodynamics in commercial TiO2 nanoparticles and ZATC-Cl.
(a) Schematic illustration for the preparation of ZATC-Cl. SEM images of (b) ZAC, (c) ZATC, and (d) ZATC-Cl.
(a, b) TEM images and (c) EDS mappings of ZATC. (d, e) TEM images, and (f) EDS mappings of ZATC-Cl.
XPS spectra of ZATC and ZATC-Cl. (a) C 1 s, (b) O 1 s, (c) Ti 2p, (d) Cl 2p.

(a) CV curves of ZATC-Cl at 0.2 mV s−1. (b) Galvanostatic charge–discharge curves of ZATC-Cl at 50 mA g−1. (c) Rate performance of commercial TiO2, ZATC, and ZATC-Cl. (d) Galvanostatic charge–discharge curves of commercial TiO2, ZATC, and ZATC-Cl. (e) Cycling performance of ZATC-Cl at 2 A g−1.

CV curves of (a) ZATC and (b) ZATC-Cl at sweep rates from 0.2 to 5 mV s−1. (c) Capacitive and diffusion-controlled contribution ratios of ZATC and ZATC-Cl at the sweep rate of 1 mV s−1. (d) Capacitive and diffusion-controlled contribution ratios of ZATC and ZATC-Cl at different sweep rates. GITT curves and corresponding Na+ diffusion coefficients of (e) ZATC and (f) ZATC-Cl.

(a) Cl 2p XPS spectra of ZATC-Cl at various charge–discharge states. The charge-density difference at (b) TiO2 (101) surface and (c) Cl-doped TiO2 (101) surface. Blue and yellow electron gas represent electric density increase and decrease, respectively. The iso-surface value is 0.02 e Å−3. Calculated DOS of (d) TiO2 and (e) Cl-doped TiO2 after sodium-ion adsorption. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The galvanostatic charge–discharge curves of ZATC-Cl//NVPC at 200 mA g−1. (b) Rate performance of ZATC-Cl//NVPC. (c) The galvanostatic charge–discharge curves of ZATC-Cl//NVPC at different current densities. (d) Cycling performance of ZATC-Cl//NVPC.
A comparison of recently reported TiO2-based anodes for SIBs.

row.entry: 143 mAh g−1 at 1.675 A g−1 after 500 cycles
144.4 mAh g−1 at 837.5 mA g−1 after 1100 cycles
TiO2-C superlattices in hollow carbon nanospheres embedded with TiO2 quantum dots [34]
200.6 mAh g−1 at 3.35 A g−1 after 1000 cycles
120 mAh g−1 at 5 A g−1 after 10,000 cycles
170 mAh g−1 at 0.67 A g−1 after 2500 cycles
N-doped carbon-coated anatase TiO2 enriched with defects [37]
168 mAh g−1 at 3.36 A g−1 after 5000 cycles
174 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1 after 5000 cycles

entry.bold: 248.5 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1 after 1000 cycles

head.ce:title: Kinetic and thermodynamic synergy of spongiform nanostructure and alien dopants enables promoted sodium-ion transfer for high-performance sodium storage

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Research Center for Renewable Energy, State Key Laboratory of Multiphase Flow in Power Engineering, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, Shannxi 710049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Research Center for Renewable Energy
State Key Laboratory of Multiphase Flow in Power Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Research Center for Renewable Energy, State Key Laboratory of Multiphase Flow in Power Engineering, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, Shannxi 710049, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xi'an Boiler&Environmental Protection Engineering Co., Ltd, Xi'an, Shannxi 710054, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Xi'an Boiler&Environmental Protection Engineering Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xi'an Boiler&Environmental Protection Engineering Co., Ltd, Xi'an, Shannxi 710054, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chlorine-doped spongiform TiO2/C composite is synthesized by an NH4Cl-assisted annealing strategy with zinc alginate and commercial TiO2 nanoparticles as precursors, demonstrating superior sodium-ion diffusion kinetics and reaction thermodynamics with well-designed spongiform structure and rational element doping.

To address the bottlenecks of sluggish sodium-ion transfer processes, the electrode materials of sodium-ion battery (SIB) are always designed from the viewpoints of sodium-ion diffusion kinetics or reaction thermodynamics for high-performance sodium storage. Herein, starting with spongiform TiO2/C composite derived from zinc alginate, a NH4Cl-assisted annealing strategy is developed to prepare chlorine-doped spongiform TiO2/C composite (ZATC-Cl). Acting as the anode of SIBs, the obtained ZATC-Cl delivers excellent sodium storage performance with a high reversible capacity of 352.4 mAh g−1 at 50 mA g−1, a superior rate capability of 246.8 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1 and a considerable high-rate cycling performance of 248.5 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1 for 1000 cycles. It is believed that the unique spongiform structure and ultra-small sized TiO2 particles in ZATC-Cl can achieve fast sodium-ion insertion/extraction, realizing rapid sodium-ion diffusion kinetics. Moreover, as well evidenced by experiment results and theoretical calculations, the Cl dopants introduced in ZATC-Cl can provide more active sites for robust sodium-ion chemical adsorption, optimizing sodium-ion reaction thermodynamics. This study demonstrates an alternative approach to improve the sodium storage capability of TiO2 anodes by the synergistically engineering the morphological and structural properties and manipulating the surface active sites, from both kinetic and thermodynamic viewpoints of sodium storage processes.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) have attracted much attention in the energy storage field for a similar mechanism with lithium-ion batteries and the natural abundance of sodium sources [1–3]. Among the developed anode materials in SIBs, TiO2 anodes show a bright future for their low cost, non-toxicity, and abundance [4–6]. Moreover, TiO2 anodes can achieve a reversible conversion between Ti4+ and Ti3+ during the sodium storage process, revealing excellent chemical stability and a high theoretical capacity of 335 mAh g−1 [7]. However, due to the sluggish sodium-ion transfer processes (i.e., sodium-ion diffusion and reaction), the sodium storage capability of developed TiO2 anodes is still far from the theoretical value, which seriously limits their application in large scale energy storage.

To conquer those limitations and thus improve the sodium storage performance of TiO2, various strategies have been developed, such as designing nanostructures [8], compositing with carbon materials [9], introducing alien elements [10], etc. Specifically, compositing with carbon materials can effectively enhance the electron conduction of TiO2 anodes, improving the rate performance [11]. In addition, constructing TiO2/C composite with an open structure can increase the contact area between TiO2 and electrolyte, decreasing sodium-ion diffusion distance and then resulting in a better sodium storage performance. Xiong et al. prepared porous carbon supported TiO2 nanoparticles (TiO2 ∩ NPCSs) by a self-template and in situ recrystallization strategy [12]. With improved electron conduction and sodium-ion transport characteristics, TiO2 ∩ NPCSs revealed an outstanding rate performance, obtaining a high reversible capacity of 101 mAh g−1 at 16.75 A g−1. Yu et al. developed a fluidized-bed plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition to coat carbon on commercial TiO2 nanoparticles [13]. Given the formation of Ti-C bonds at the interface between TiO2 and carbon, the obtained TiO2/C composite showed an improved electric conductivity, delivering a reversible capacity of 116.9 mAh g−1 at 4 A g−1. In our previous study, ultra-small TiO2 nanoparticles embedded in carbon nanosheets (TCNS) were successfully constructed through an annealing-etching two-step process with carbon-coated Zn-Ti layered double hydroxide as precursors [14]. Benefited from the ultra-small TiO2 particle size and nanosheet structure, TCNS achieved rapid sodium-ion insertion/extraction, exhibiting an impressive rate performance of 197.2 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1. Although the rate performance of TiO2 anodes can be enhanced by compositing with carbon materials, the TiO2/C composites have generally suffered from the large irreversible capacities during the initial discharge process for the irreversible sodium-ion capture in carbon materials [15]. Therefore, further modifications are required by the TiO2/C composites to unlock the great potentials for practical applications in SIBs.

It has also been well documented that the introduction of alien elements into TiO2 could effectively improve the sodium storage performances, with the modulated electronic structures increasing the electric conductivity of TiO2. Various elements, such as N [16], Fe [17], Nb [18], and Sn [19], etc., have been introduced into TiO2 anodes, and without exception, excellent sodium storage performances were achieved over those doped TiO2 anodes. For instance, Wang et al. designed sulfur-doped anatase TiO2 nanoparticles anchored on a large-area carbon sheet (S−TiO2/CS), delivering a high reversible capacity of 293.5 mAh g−1 at 167.5 mA g−1, due to the enhanced electric conductivity caused by the lower electronegativity of sulfur and the less ionic nature of the Ti−S bond [20]. Lu et al. developed a defect-induced selective surface doping strategy to dope phosphorus into the TiO2@TiO2−x core@shell architecture [21]. Given the enhanced electron conduction and sodium-ion transport in the surface-functionalized TiO2−x-P thin layer, the obtained TiO2@TiO2−x-P achieved a reversible capacity of 303 mAh g−1 at 50 mA g−1 with a high initial coulombic efficiency of 64.7%. These achievements clearly demonstrate that alien element doping can enhance the electric conductivity of TiO2 and effectively increase the reversibility of sodium storage processes of TiO2 anodes.

As inspired by the above observations, to address the bottlenecks of sluggish sodium-ion transfer processes (i.e., sodium-ion diffusion and reaction), herein, starting with the spongiform TiO2/C composite (ZATC) derived from zinc alginate, a novel NH4Cl-assisted annealing strategy was developed to dope chlorine (Cl) into ZATC. Benefited from the spongiform structure with improved electron transport, the Cl-doped spongiform TiO2/C composite (ZATC-Cl) achieved fast sodium-ion insertion/extraction processes and revealed accelerated sodium-ion diffusion kinetics (Fig. 1 a). Moreover, the Cl dopants were evidenced to act as active sites enabling the robust sodium-ion chemical adsorption, displaying preferable sodium-ion reaction thermodynamics (Fig. 1b). In the synergy of optimized sodium-ion transfer kinetics and thermodynamics, ZATC-Cl presented impressive sodium storage performance. Compared to commercial TiO2 nanoparticles and ZATC, the as-prepared ZATC-Cl exhibited superior sodium storage capability, achieving a high reversible capacity of 352.4 mAh g−1 at 50 mA g−1, even maintaining 246.8 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1. Even after 1000 cycles, the ZATC-Cl anode still delivered a high reversible capacity of 248.5 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1, signifying the remarkable cycling performance. By assembling the ZATC-Cl anode into a full cell with carbon-coated Na3V2(PO4)3 (NVPC) as cathode, the ZATC-Cl//NVPC full cell demonstrated excellent sodium storage performance, delivering an energy density of 95.2 Wh kg−1 at a high power density of 1044.9 W kg−1.

To prepare ZATC, commercial TiO2 nanoparticles were uniformly dispersed in sodium alginate solution under vigorous stirring with citric acid added as the binder. After an ion-exchange and carbonization process, ZATC was successfully obtained (Fig. 2 a). Similar to the zinc alginate derived carbon (ZAC) prepared via the same ion-exchange and carbonization process without commercial TiO2 nanoparticles added (Fig. 2b), the obtained ZATC presents a spongiform structure, with three-dimensional carbon network formed by cross-linked carbon fibers and TiO2 nanoparticles uniformly dispersed in carbon network (Fig. 2c). In comparison to ZAC, ZATC exhibits much increased sodium storage performances; moreover, by varying the amounts of TiO2 in ZATC, the sodium storage performance is optimized, with the highest capacity reaching 187.9 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1 (Fig. S1 and related discussions). Then, Cl was doped into ZATC by simply annealing ZATC with NH4Cl in a solid-phase mixture (Fig. 2a). The resulted ZATC-Cl keeps the spongiform structure unchanged (Fig. 2d), with sodium storage performance significantly improved, as compared to ZATC. Moreover, by well controlling the annealing temperatures of Cl doping, the optimized sodium storage capacity could reach as high as 269.7 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1 (Fig. S2 and related discussions). Thus, in the following discussions and analysis, ZATC and ZATC-Cl were obtained with the amounts of TiO2 nanoparticles and the annealing temperatures of Cl doping optimized, if without specifically noted.

The porosity structures of ZATC and ZATC-Cl were then analyzed by the nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms (Fig. S3a), with specific surface areas calculated to be 303.6 m2 g−1 and 263.5 m2 g−1, respectively. The slightly reduced specific surface area after Cl doping is related to the partial destruction of the porous structure of ZATC triggered by the grinding and mixing of ZATC and NHCl4. Moreover, ZATC and ZATC-Cl reveal very similar pore size distribution, with average pore sizes mainly located at ∼ 3.6 nm (Fig. S3b). These observations indicate that the spongiform structure of ZATC could be well maintained after annealing with NH4Cl. Thereon, it is easily accepted that ZATC and ZATC-Cl with the well-developed spongiform structure could achieve sufficient contact with the electrolyte, shortening the diffusion distance of sodium ions and accelerating the sodium-ion diffusion kinetics. Moreover, the three-dimensional carbon network ensures the high electric conductivity for the enhanced electron transport in ZATC and ZATC-Cl, which will benefit sodium storage.

The structural properties of ZATC and ZATC-Cl were further investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As revealed in XRD patterns (Fig. S4), TiO2 nanoparticles in anatase phase (JCPDS No.21-1272) are identified in both ZATC and ZATC-Cl, indicating that the NH4Cl-assisted annealing process would not change the crystal structure of TiO2 in ZATC-Cl. TEM images confirm the very similar morphology and structures of ZATC and ZATC-Cl, in which TiO2 nanoparticles are well dispersed in the carbon network (Fig. 3 a, d), with the clear lattice spacing of 0.352 nm well indexed by the (101) plane of anatase TiO2 (Fig. 3b, e) [22]. Moreover, the ultra-small size of TiO2 nanoparticles (5 ∼ 10 nm) implies that the sodium-ion diffusion distance could be significantly shortened, benefiting the fast sodium storage. The energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) mappings again verify the similar structure characteristics of ZATC and ZATC-Cl, with TiO2 nanoparticles distributed in the carbon network (Fig. 3c, f). It is worth noting that the spatial distribution of elemental Cl is consistent well with Ti and O (Fig. 3f), indicating the successful introduction of Cl into TiO2 nanoparticles in ZATC-Cl through the heat treatment of ZATC with NH4Cl.

Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis was carried out in air atmosphere to reveal the chemical compositions of ZATC and ZATC-Cl. A weight loss of 25.56 wt% could be observed for ZATC depending on the temperature increasing to 600 °C, with carbon content calculated to be 25.56 wt% in ZATC (Fig. S5). In comparison, ZATC-Cl shows the weight loss increased to 32.76 wt%, possibly related to the Cl doping into TiO2 during the NH4Cl treatment. With the atomic ratio of Cl:Ti in ZATC-Cl determined to be 0.14:1 (Table S1), confirming the successful introduction of Cl into ZATC through NH4Cl treatment, the element chemical states in ZATC and ZATC-Cl were further explored by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As presented in Fig. 4 a, the C 1 s spectra of ZATC and ZATC-Cl can be divided into three peaks corresponding to the C–C (284.8 eV), C-O (285.6 eV), and C=O (286.8 eV) bonds, respectively [23,24]. There is no extra peak in C 1 s spectra of ZATC-Cl, indicating that C does not bond with Cl in ZATC-Cl. In the O 1 s spectra of ZATC and ZATC-Cl (Fig. 4b), the peaks at 530.8 and 532.6 eV are ascribed to the O-Ti and O-C bonds, respectively, indicating that the NH4Cl-assisted annealing process would not change the chemical states of O [25]. In the Ti 2p spectra, the peaks at 459.2 and 464.6 eV are attributed to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 of Ti-O bonds (Fig. 4c) for ZATC, respectively [26]. In comparison, two additional peaks at 457.0 and 461.7 eV are detected in the Ti 2p spectrum of ZATC-Cl, implying the formation of Ti-Cl bonds after annealing with NH4Cl. Furthermore, in the Cl 2p spectrum of ZATC-Cl (Fig. 4d), the peaks of 199.3 and 200.8 eV are ascribed to the Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2 of Ti-Cl bonds, respectively, clearly confirming the existence of Ti-Cl bonds [27]. The above XPS analysis demonstrates that Cl dopants have been successfully introduced into ZATC-Cl, with strong chemical bonding formed between Cl and Ti through the NH4Cl-assisted annealing process.

To evaluate the sodium storage performance of ZATC-Cl, ZATC-Cl was assembled in a coin-type cell with sodium metal as the counter-electrode. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of ZATC-Cl were recorded at a sweep rate of 0.2 mV s−1 in the voltage range of 0.01 ∼ 3.0 V vs. Na+/Na (Fig. 5 a). In the first cycle, a broad cathode peak between 0.6 and 1.2 V is ascribed to electrolyte decomposition to form the solid electrolyte interface (SEI). [28] The irreversible peak at about 0.01 V is attributed to sodium-ion irreversible caption in carbon materials and irreversible conversion of Ti4+ to Ti0 [29,30]. Subsequently, the cathodic and anodic peaks at 0.58/0.90 V can be observed in the second and third cycles, corresponding to the reversible Ti4+/Ti3+ redox reactions. The well overlapped CV curves in the second and third cycles indicate the good electrochemical reversibility of ZATC-Cl.

Galvanostatic charge–discharge curves of ZATC-Cl were measured at a current density of 50 mA g−1 in the voltage range of 0.01 ∼ 3.0 V vs. Na+/Na (Fig. 5b). In the first cycle, the ZATC-Cl electrode reveals a high reversible capacity of 390.9 mAh g−1, which exceeds the theoretical capacity of TiO2 (335 mAh g−1), implying that Cl doping brings more sodium storage reactions. The high reversible capacity of ZATC-Cl is also ascribed to the unique spongiform structure and ultra-small TiO2 particle size, which shortens sodium-ion diffusion distance and achieves fast and sufficient sodium-ion insertion/extraction processes. Due to the irreversible conversion of Ti4+ to Ti0, the irreversible sodium-ion capture in the carbon network, and the electrolyte decomposition, the ZATC-Cl electrode yields a large irreversible capacity in the first cycle and exhibits a low initial coulombic efficiency of 40.6% [29–31]. In the second and third cycles, the ZATC-Cl displays close reversible capacities of 356.5 and 350.4 mAh g−1, respectively, indicating the good sodium storage stability of ZATC-Cl.

Then, the rate capability of ZATC-Cl was evaluated at various current densities, with ZATC and commercial TiO2 as references. As shown in Fig. 5c, Commercial TiO2 displays very poor sodium storage performance, with reversible capacities reaching only 202.8, 178.6, 164.9, 151.3, 142.2, and 133.4 mAh g−1 at 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 2 A g−1, respectively. Benefited from the unique spongiform structure, ZATC achieves fast sodium-ions insertion/extraction kinetics, obtaining reversible capacities of 274.7, 257.2, 242.2, 220.7, 203.6, and 186.8 mAh g−1 at 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 2 A g−1, respectively. In comparison to commercial TiO2 and ZATC, ZATC-Cl reveals much superior sodium storage performance, displaying high reversible capacities of 352.4, 331.0, 319.9, 294.0, 273.0, and 246.8 mAh g−1 at 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 2 A g−1, respectively. As further demonstrated by galvanostatic charge–discharge curves (Fig. 5d), ZATC-Cl exhibits significantly increased capacity in 1.8 ∼ 3.0 V, as compared to commercial TiO2 and ZATC, confirming the increase of sodium storage reactions after Cl doping. According to the above discussion, it is hypothesized that the excellent sodium storage performance of ZATC-Cl may attribute to the optimized sodium-ion diffusion kinetics and reaction thermodynamics (Fig. 1), given that the spongiform structure accelerates rapid sodium-ion insertion/extraction and Cl doping increases sodium storage reactions, respectively.

To investigate the sodium storage stability of ZATC-Cl, the long cycling measurement was performed at 2 A g−1 (Fig. 5e). After 1000 cycles, ZATC-Cl still exhibits a high reversible capacity of 248.5 mAh g−1, without obvious decay. More encouragingly, it is observable that ZATC-Cl reveals much better high-rate cycling performance, as compared with the recently reported TiO2-based anodes (Table 1 ) [32–38]. During the cycling measurement of ZATC-Cl, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were also recorded to explore the stability of ZATC-Cl (Fig. S6). In the initial cycles, the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) decreases from 146.2 Ω to 33.08 Ω, related to the formation of SEI and the gradual stabilization of the solid–liquid interface. Then, the Rct of ZATC-Cl maintains well after 500 cycles (Rct = 37.18 Ω), confirming the excellent cycling stability of ZATC-Cl. After 1000 cycles, the charge transfer resistance increases to 67 Ω, which is related to the thickened SEI and is a common phenomenon in cycling tests of SIBs [25].

For the in-depth understanding of the electrochemical behaviors of ZATC and ZATC-Cl during sodium storage processes, CV curves were measured at different sweep rates to reveal the sodium-ion diffusion kinetics (Fig. 6 a, b). Surprisingly, the CV curves of ZATC-Cl show higher currents than ZATC in the voltage range of 2.0 ∼ 3.0 V at various sweep rates from 0.2 to 5 mV s−1 (Fig. S7), which can be reminded to the discussions in Fig. 5d, again indicating Cl doping increases sodium storage reactions. Generally, the total charge storage of the electrode can divide into capacitive and diffusion-controlled charge storage, depending on Eq. (1): [39]
(1)
i
v
=
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 2
where the current i(v) is determined by the capacitive (k1v) and the diffusion-controlled (k2v1/2 ) processes. One would note that ZATC-Cl presents a capacitive contribution ratio (73.0%) lower than ZATC (77.5%) (Fig. 6c), which suggests that Cl doping increases the diffusion-controlled sodium storage reaction for ZATC-Cl. With the increasing sweep rates, the capacitive contribution ratios of ZATC and ZATC-Cl gradually increase from 65.3% and 59.7% at 0.2 mV s−1 to 84.2% and 82.1% at 2 mV s−1, respectively (Fig. 6d). Such high capacitive contributions at various sweep rates indicate that ZATC and ZATC-Cl can achieve fast sodium storage processes, reflecting the fast sodium-ion diffusion kinetics of ZATC and ZATC-Cl (Fig. 1a). Furthermore, it is found that the diffusion-controlled ratios of ZATC-Cl at each sweep rate are higher than ZATC, confirming Cl doping can increase diffusion-controlled sodium storage reaction for ZATC-Cl, also indicating that ZATC-Cl may have preferable sodium-ion reaction thermodynamics (Fig. 1b).

Then, the sodium-ion diffusion coefficient (DNa+ ) of ZATC and ZATC-Cl can be obtained by Eq. (2), depending on the galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) [40].
(2)
D
Na
+
=
4
π τ
m
V m
MA
2
Δ
E s
Δ
E τ
2
in which m, M, Vm , A, and τ represent mass, molar mass, molar volume, active surface area, and pulse duration, respectively. ΔEτ and ΔEs are voltage changes of pulse process and steady-state, respectively. At different charge–discharge states, ZATC and ZATC-Cl exhibit the DNa+ values in the range of 10−9 ∼ 10−10 cm2 s−1, which are higher than many TiO2-based anodes (Fig. 6e,f) [41–43]. It is worth noting that the DNa+ values of ZATC-Cl are slightly lower than ZATC, which is related to Cl doping. Specifically, Cl doping can increase the diffusion-controlled sodium storage reaction, resulting in higher diffusion-controlled contribution ratios (Fig. 6d). Thus, the sodium storage process of ZATC-Cl suffers more diffusion limitations than ZATC, leading to lower DNa+ values. Anyhow, such high DNa+ values of ZATC-Cl and ZATC should be attributable to the unique spongiform structure and ultra-small sized TiO2 particles, which shortens sodium-ion diffusion distance and achieves fast sodium-ion insertion/extraction processes, demonstrating the superior sodium-ion diffusion kinetics.

In general, oxygen atoms in TiO2 acts as sodium storage active sites, which could be evidenced by the reversible conversion of O 1 s spectra of ZATC and ZATC-Cl during sodium storage processes (Fig. S8 and related discussions). To deepen the understanding of the pivotal role of Cl doping contributing to the excellent sodium storage performance, Cl 2p XPS spectra of ZATC-Cl were recorded at various charge–discharge states (Fig. 7 a). For the primal ZATC-Cl, Cl 2p XPS spectrum exhibits two peaks located at 199.3 eV (Cl 2p3/2) and 200.8 eV (Cl 2p1/2), referring to Ti-Cl bonds. When ZATC-Cl is discharged to 0.01 V, the binding energies of Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2 increase to 199.9 and 201.4 eV, respectively, indicating that Cl dopants may bond with sodium-ion and act as active sites during the sodium storage processes. With the ZATC-Cl electrode is recharged to 3.0 V, the binding energies of Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2 return to 199.3 and 200.8 eV, respectively. These results verify that except for oxygen atoms, the Cl dopants also act as active sites, which could realize a reversible conversion during sodium storage processes. Furthermore, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed to theoretically evidence that the Cl dopants in ZATC-Cl act as the active sites for sodium storage reaction, by monitoring the adsorption processes of sodium ions at TiO2 and Cl-doped TiO2. As presented in Fig. 7b and 7c, sodium-ion is surrounded by more free electron gas at the (101) surface of Cl-doped TiO2 than TiO2, indicating that Na preferentially bonds with Cl rather than O by forming stronger electronic interactions. Similar phenomenon happens in the case that more sodium ions (e.g., two sodium ions) are absorbed at TiO2 and Cl-doped TiO2 (Fig. S9), with stronger electronic interactions remaining in Cl-doped TiO2 than TiO2. As further demonstrated in the calculated density of states (DOS) of TiO2 and Cl-doped TiO2 after sodium-ion adsorption (Fig. 7d, e), Cl-doped TiO2 reveals a narrower bandgap, implying an increased electric conductivity, as related to TiO2, which is conducive to facilitate the sodium storage reactions. By integrating the part density states (PDOS) to the Fermi level, Cl-doped TiO2 shows Ti integral area and total integral area larger than TiO2, suggesting that Cl-doped TiO2 have more free electrons and preferable electron conduction. Meanwhile, the smaller Na integral area observed for Cl-doped TiO2 than TiO2 indicates that Na would form stronger interactions with Cl than O, agreeing well with the previous analysis in XPS (Fig. 7a) and charge-density difference (Fig. 7b, c). Hence, all the above experimental and theoretical results clearly verify that Cl dopants can act as active sites of sodium storage reactions and achieve robust sodium-ion chemical adsorption, resulting in preferable sodium-ion reaction thermodynamics of ZATC-Cl.

Based on the above discussions, it is clear that ZATC-Cl possesses fast sodium-ion diffusion kinetics and preferable sodium-ion reaction thermodynamics during the sodium storage process. With the synergy of superior sodium-ion transfer kinetics and thermodynamics, ZATC-Cl achieves a remarkable reversible capacity of 352.4 mAh g−1 at 50 mA g−1, an outstanding rate capability of 246.8 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1, and a considerable high-rate cycling performance of 248.5 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1 for 1000 cycles.

To explore the potential of ZATC-Cl for practical application in SIB, ZATC-Cl as the anode was assembled into a full cell with carbon-coated Na3V2(PO4)3 (NVPC) as the cathode. With superior ionic conduction (Fig. S10 and related discussions), the NVPC cathode displays excellent rate performance, delivering a reversible capacity of 113 mAh g−1 at 23.4 mA g−1 and maintaining 99 mAh g−1 at 1.17 A g−1 (Fig. S11). Besides, the NVPC cathode exhibits a steady sodium storage voltage plateau at 3.4 V, which is beneficial to achieve a high energy density (Fig. S12) [44–46]. For the assembled ZATC-Cl//NVPC full cell, a high reversible capacity of 270.8 mAh g−1 could be obtained at 200 mA g−1 (Fig. 8 a). Even at 2 A g−1, the ZATC-Cl//NVPC full cell can still achieve a considerable reversible capacity reaching 181.8 mAh g−1 as high (Fig. 8b), displaying its outstanding rate performance. With the increasing current density, the full cell maintains a stable voltage platform at ∼ 2.5 V (Fig. 8c), which is beneficial to maintain the energy density with increasing power density. After 200 cycles, the full cell still holds a capacity of 134.2 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1 (Fig. 8d), with a capacity retention of 75.5%. The declined capacity of ZATC-Cl//NVPC full cell is related to the poor cycling performance of NVPC cathode and the irreversible sodium-ion capture during cycles (Fig. S13 and related discussions). The energy density (E) and power density (P) of the full cell can be calculated by Eqs. (3) and (4):
(3)
E =
∫
t 1
t 2
I V d t
(4)
P =
E t
where I, V, t1 , and t2 represent current density, voltage, the start time of discharge process, and the end time of discharge process, respectively. Then, a high energy density of 148.5 Wh kg−1 could be achieved for the ZATC-Cl//NVPC full cell, at a power density of 110.8 W kg−1. Impressively, when the power density increases to 1044.9 W kg−1, the ZATC-Cl//NVPC full cell can still reach an energy density as high as 95.2 Wh kg−1. These results demonstrate that ZATC-Cl could serve as a promising anode candidate for assembling full cell SIBs with excellent sodium storage capability [47–49]. The excellent sodium storage performance and promising application prospect of ZATC-Cl should give credit to the synergy of optimized sodium-ion diffusion kinetics and reaction thermodynamics with well-designed spongiform structure and rational element doping.

In summary, spongiform TiO2/C composite (ZATC), synthesized with commercial TiO2 nanoparticles and zinc alginate as the carbon source, was successfully doped with Cl through a novel NH4Cl-assisted annealing strategy. Benefiting from the spongiform structure and the ultra-small sized TiO2 particles, the obtained ZATC-Cl acting as anode achieves fast sodium ions insertion/extraction, accelerating sodium-ion diffusion kinetics. Moreover, the Cl dopants introduced in ZATC-Cl create more active sites for robust sodium-ion chemical adsorption, achieving preferable sodium-ion reaction thermodynamics. Given the synergy of optimized sodium-ion transfer kinetics and thermodynamics, ZATC-Cl displays remarkable sodium storage capability. Specifically, ZATC-Cl exhibits a high reversible capacity of 352.4 mAh g−1 at 50 mA g−1 and an outstanding rate performance of 246.8 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1. Notably, ZATC-Cl also shows a considerable high-rate cycling performance with reversible capacity maintained at 248.5 mAh g−1 after 1000 cycles at 2 A g−1, which outperforms most of the previously reported TiO2 based anodes. Furthermore, the assembled ZATC-Cl//NVPC full cell can deliver an energy density of 95.2 Wh kg−1 at a high power density of 1044.9 W kg−1. This study develops a novel strategy combining nanostructure construction and element doping to optimize the sodium storage performance of TiO2 anodes, and gives guidelines in designing high-performance electrode materials from the viewpoints of sodium-ion transfer kinetics and thermodynamics.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFE0193900) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51961165103). S. Shen was supported by “the Youth Innovation Teams of Shaanxi Universities”, the National Program for Support of Top-notch Young Professionals and the “Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.”

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
